sub_discipline: Lagrangian ocean analysis / computational physical oceanography
sub_discipline_context: Lagrangian ocean analysis, whereby virtual particles are tracked within the flow field of hydrodynamic models, has over the last two decades increasingly been used by physical oceanographers and marine biologists alike
integration_scheme: Fourth-order Runge-Kutta by default; Euler-forward and adaptive Runge-Kutta-Fehlberg (RKF45) also provided
integration_scheme_context: In Parcels, the trajectory Eq. (1) is by default time-stepped using a fourth-order Runge-Kutta scheme, although schemes for Euler-forward and adaptive Runge-Kutta-Fehlberg integration (RKF45, e.g. Alexander, 1990) are also provided.
time_step_strategy: Fixed user-specified time step passed to pset.execute; an interval keyword supports secondary sub-timestepping for coupling. Adaptive stepping is available via the RKF45 scheme.
time_step_strategy_context: This method requires as input the list of kernels, the start time of the execution loop, the runtime of the execution loop, the Runge-Kutta integration time step (here taken to be 5 min) ... Parcels' dynamic update loop also provides an interval keyword to impose a secondary sub-timestepping ... schemes for Euler-forward and adaptive Runge-Kutta-Fehlberg integration (RKF45 ...) are also provided.
time_step_value: 5 min RK4 time step in the foraminifera example and in all seven idealized test cases; peninsula illustration used 30 s
time_step_value_context: the Runge-Kutta integration time step (here taken to be 5 min) ... All test cases are run with Runga-Kutta4 (RK4) integration and in JIT mode ... 100 particles are advected for 20 h with a time-step size of 30 s.
interpolation_spatial: Linear interpolation on regular grids (linear and nearest-neighbour available in SciPy debug mode); only regular grids supported in v0.9
interpolation_spatial_context: In the SciPy debug mode this is facilitated by scipy.interpolate.RegularGridInterpolator objects and supports linear and nearest-neighbour interpolation ... Currently, only linear interpolation schemes are implemented in Parcels, both in space and in time. In space, Parcels can currently only work on regular grids
interpolation_temporal_context: Currently, only linear interpolation schemes are implemented in Parcels, both in space and in time.
diffusion_scheme: Brownian motion / random walk via a normal-variate random-number kernel with uniform horizontal diffusivity K_h (custom user kernel)
diffusion_scheme_context: The test case of Brownian motion with uniform K h tests for the accuracy and implementation of the random number generator. Here, a total of 100 000 particles are seeded at the origin of a (60 x 60) km grid centred around the origin with zero velocities, and then diffused using a normal variate random number distribution with K h = 100 m 2 s -1 . ... in the presence of turbulence, a random walk kernel or Brownian motion is required
software: Parcels v0.9 (Probably A Really Computationally Efficient Lagrangian Simulator)
software_context: Here, we describe a novel framework for computing Lagrangian particle trajectories, named Parcels ("Probably A Really Computationally Efficient Lagrangian Simulator"). ... We have decided to brand this version of Parcels as v0.9
ocean_model: OFES (Masumoto et al., 2004) for the worked example; idealized/analytical flow fields for validation test cases
ocean_model_context: The hydrodynamic fields that carry the foraminifera come from the OFES model (Masumoto et al., 2004) ... Three-dimensional velocities and temperature are available on 1/10 horizontal resolution, on 54 vertical levels, and are stored as 3-day averages.
methods_detail: Parcels is a Python framework with high-level API; uses domain-specific-language paradigm with runtime C code generation and JIT compilation for kernels. Particles are defined via Particle/ParticleSet classes and updated with composable Kernel functions (signature KernelName(particle, fieldset, time, dt)); fields stored in Field/FieldSet objects loaded from NetCDF (three consecutive time slices held in memory). Two execution modes: SciPy debug mode and JIT mode (ScipyParticle vs JITParticle). Supports forward and time-backward integration; error-recovery kernels (e.g. OutOfBoundsError). Worked example re-implements Van Sebille et al. (2015) foraminifera tracking off Cape Town (17.3 E, 34.7 S, 2440 m depth), using AdvectionRK4_3D plus custom Sink, Age, Sample and DeleteParticle kernels, with sinking at 200 m/day and 50 m dwelling depth.
methods_detail_context: Parcels is based on the domain-specific languages paradigm ... This approach is based on automated code generation at runtime ... JIT mode Runtime code generation and just-in-time compilation (JIT) are utilized to generate low-level C code ... The execution mode of the particle update loop is determined by the type of the particle (ScipyParticle or JITParticle) ... We need to define four custom kernels: one that causes the particle to sink after it dies, one that keeps track of its age ... concatenated with the AdvectionRK4_3D kernel ... the foraminifera were tracked back as they sunk at 200 m per day ... at their 50 m dwelling depth.
code_tracking_software: Parcels source code (Python, with runtime-generated C); hosted on GitHub with Travis CI continuous integration; project website www.oceanparcels.org
code_tracking_software_context: Every push of code changes to GitHub automatically triggers a validation of the entire codebase ... through the www.travis-ci.org web service ... we will provide a properly referenced library of user-contributed kernels for others to reuse on www.oceanparcels.org.
code_experiment_setup: yes, full Python code for the foraminifera worked example and for all seven idealized test cases is archived at https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.823994, including the get_ofesdata_agulhas.sh download script
code_experiment_setup_context: The full Python code for this experiment in Parcels is available at https://doi.org/ 10.5281/zenodo.823994. ... The Python code for all test cases is available at https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo. 823994. ... The bash script get_ofesdata_agulhas.sh provided at https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.823994
code_analysis: Parcels provides a utility script convert_IndexedOutputToArray to convert Indexed NetCDF output to array format; trajectories plotted with matplotlib and Basemap
code_analysis_context: Parcels provides a utility script convert_IndexedOutputToArray to convert an Indexed NetCDF file to array format. The particle trajectories can then be plotted using the matplotlib and Basemap libraries
config_available: yes, key experimental parameters (domain sizes, grid resolutions, particle counts, RK4 time steps, output intervals, K_h, sinking speed, dwelling depth, etc.) are specified in the text, and full Python configuration scripts are archived at the Zenodo DOI
config_available_context: the Runge-Kutta integration time step (here taken to be 5 min), the interval at which output is written (here once per day) ... 100 000 particles are seeded at the origin of a (60 x 60) km grid ... K h = 100 m 2 s -1 ... The full Python code for this experiment in Parcels is available at https://doi.org/ 10.5281/zenodo.823994.
passage_locations: Section 2 (Prototype design and philosophy, including Advection algorithm, Custom kernels, Execution and JIT compilation, Interpolation, External field data); Section 3 (worked example in the Agulhas region); Section 4 (Model evaluation with seven idealized test cases); Section 5 (Future outlook)
extraction_notes: Paper is a software/framework description of Parcels v0.9. Numerical core: RK4 (default) with Euler-forward and RKF45 optional; linear interpolation in space and time on regular grids only. Diffusion is not a built-in scheme but demonstrated via a user-defined Brownian-motion kernel in test case 4.2.7. Ocean model for the real-world example is OFES; validation uses analytical velocity fields generated inside Python. Code and data are released via Zenodo DOI 10.5281/zenodo.823994, GitHub (with Travis CI) and oceanparcels.org. The paper does not report on energetics, specific parallelization (v0.9 is serial), or unstructured-mesh support (deferred to v1.0).
